data.title: Comcast: The P/E Ratio Is Crazy Low, Is It A Buy
data.description:
Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA) stock took a dramatic 10% plunge the day it released its Q4 and full year 2024 results. Since then, the stock has managed to claw back a little under half of the losses. Since its earnings ratio is now in the single digits, many are questioning if it's finally time to dive in.

Memo To Management: Never Overpromise

Considering that the numbers themselves really weren’t that bad, the proximate cause of the decline appears to have been the erroneous estimates management themselves had previously released. Comcast leadership had indicated that it expected broadband losses of 100k give or take just weeks prior to New Years; it wound up reporting losing 139,000 broadband subs, which meant that the last month of 2024 must have been just horrible.

It’s true that Hurricanes Milton and Helene hit in Q4, but it seems unlikely that was the cause of the overshoot. Those hurricanes were less violent than Hurricane Ian, the Florida hurricane of 2022 and one of the worst in recorded history. Comcast put broadband losses from that natural disaster at 22,000 total. Management had indicated that they expected these hurricane losses to only be around 10,000.

Author History

It was almost nine years ago, I wrote an article arguing that Comcast no longer represented a good investment, given the changes coming in the Pay-TV system.

At the time, I was concerned primarily with the rather esoteric issue of FCC regulatory initiatives pertaining to the consumer pricing of carrier-branded set-top box equipment…..and if that topic doesn’t put you to sleep, nothing will.

My choice of approach may be questionable, but my results proved solid. Since that article, Comcast has risen a grand total of 13%; the S&P 500 has nearly tripled over the same time frame. I have periodically returned to it since then, trying to keep readers updated on the many issues touching on a corporate behemoth as large as Comcast, but my conclusion has never really altered; Comcast is a troubled stock because its monopoly is collapsing.

That is not only true of its pay-TV monopoly; which was never a complete monopoly and is now in any case so small in margins that Comcast hardly cares what happens to it. It is also true of Comcast’s real power center, the broadband business, which I noted all the way back in 2017 was going to face real trouble as new generations of wireless network technologies came online.

That, not hurricanes, is what almost certainly lies at the core of the disappointing number. Comcast management simply continues to underestimate the impact of widespread deployment of fixed-wireless.

Comcast's Biggest Draw: It's P/E Ratio

The primary argument in favor of keeping Comcast in the portfolio is that it doesn’t need to preserve current profit levels to be a good investment. With the latest decline, Comcast now trades at an almost ridiculous 8.8 TTM P/E ratio. The S&P 500 is 3x that. There are many who argue that Comcast simply can’t fall any further, and it’s time to buy in.

At the outset, it's important to note that the S&P 500 itself has been on a rocket-rollercoaster since the election in November and that 28 P/E ratio itself may be due to come crashing back at some point. But it’s fair to note that even if we compare Comcast’s ratio to a more standard 22 P/E it still looks ludicrously undervalued.

It is also important to keep in mind that Comcast is one of the more heavily leveraged stocks out there. At a debt load of $105 billion, its bondholders account for almost half of its Enterprise Value, roughly 44%. If the broader market reverts to pre-election levels and takes Comcast with it, that ratio could climb over 50%.

Having said all of that, the point is still well-taken; Comcast has ample cushion for shareholders even if earnings take a hit. With bondholders being paid roughly $4.1 billion per year and leaving the other $16 billion to the shareholders, Comcast could afford to see its EBIT fall 30% and still see its stock double, assuming the EV/EBIT multiple reverts to normal.

Spinoff Implications Remain Uncertain

The key question is, can Comcast limit the damage in that way? After years of arguing against the current that Comcast’s cash flow was not as secure as it looked, most of the people I talk to now accept that Comcast will continue to see pressure on its operating margins as fixed-wireless and streaming continue to eat into its broadband and TV operations. But can it hold enough of those pies, combined with continued growth in its mobile operations, to ride out the rough seas and take its stock back up?

Unlike AT&T (T) Comcast does not intend to liquidate its TV operations and take the cash. Rather, it is spinning off the cable networks to its shareholders in a new company. I am not convinced that meaningful value should attach to that asset. Comcast management itself admitted that those networks represent only 2% of Peacock viewing. Some of that could be put down to the fact that most national news is still consumed on linear, not streaming; that statistic may not be the best prism through which to view the value of CNBC and MSNBC.

But it probably does capture the relative values of the sports and scripted elements. As for reality, well, Peacock is in many ways the most reality-friendly of all the streaming services. Any reality content that can’t get traction on Peacock probably doesn’t have much value.

For this article, I am going to assume that the spinoff has a net value of $0. That is almost certainly too harsh; even I think it has some value. But my goal here will be to put a value on the main company; investors can then plug in for themselves whatever spinoff value they think is appropriate to come up with a final number.

NBCUniversal Remains A Bright Spot, But...

Spinoff aside, the media division of Comcast should continue to perform well. But its current margins may still come in for some degree of pressure. The key concern for any media company in the United States right now is that the collapse of the linear bundle is accelerating, and many questions if margins can be preserved in the far more competitive arena of streaming. Comcast does not break out its traditional cable revenue separately from its vMVPD and streaming revenue, nor does it break out broadcast versus cable networks.

However, we do know that this year, Peacock increased revenues by $1.5 billion and EBITDA by $900 million, while total media EBITDA increased only $175 million with the Olympics profits included. Since Olympics profit came to $450 million, EBITDA would have decline $275 million without it, suggesting that last year turning linear revenues into streaming revenues haircut profits by $275 million.

That is a highly imprecise way of estimating, and even I don’t entirely trust it since, as I’ve explained before, the way studios partition revenues and costs between streaming and linear has little rhyme or reason to it, and doesn’t always reflect economic realities. Still, if cord-cutting and attendant Peacock expansion were summing to $275 million a year of declines, four more years of such declines would chop $1.1 billion off of Comcast’s net income.

Then there's the new NBA deal. While many have seen it as a triumphant return for Comcast to the only sport that can rival the NFL on social media, I have noted before that it could produce losses of as much as $1 billion a year over the life of the 11-year contract.

The worst-case scenario for NBCUniversal is if there is a large-scale reversion to mean pricing in the linear TV advertising market. I examined this in depth in a prior article, so I won't be covering all that again. In summary, it is less likely than not, but if it did happen, the hit would probably be in the multi-billion dollar range.

Broadband Broadsides

Comcast’s key business remains domestic broadband, which is generating $26.2 billion in revenue off of a customer base of 31.8 million. At almost $70 per month, Comcast is continuing to charge customers considerably more than wireless competitors Verizon (VZ) and T-Mobile (TMUS) do, with their charges officially at $50 per month but unofficially often available for $35 to their mobile customer base.

The difficulty here for Comcast is that wireline internet is overwhelmingly a fixed-cost business; when Comcast loses a customer its network doesn’t become hardly any less expensive to maintain.

It all comes down to how much Comcast has to cut this number to preserve its broadband business. Were it to cut it all the way to $50, the loss of over 25% of broadband revenue would eat almost $7 billion off of Comcast’s income and make its seemingly bargain-bin price a whole lot less appealing.

Self-Sorting Is Self-Defeating

Comcast management has regularly insisted that it has no intention of doing this because it does not intend to fight T-Mobile and Verizon for every customer. Rather, it intends to let the value-oriented bottom end of the market go to the cheaper services, while it aims to preserve market position at the middle- and higher-end price points.

There is a certain logic to this. Charter (CHTR) reports that cord-cutters are consuming nearly 800 GB per month of data - T-Mobile and Verizon report consumption levels well, well below that, suggesting that indeed it is the data-sippers, at least in relative terms, who are defecting and that heavy data users trust the big pipes of cable more than they desire the low prices of wireless. Comcast management has explicitly stated that their strategy is to wait for Verizon and T-Mobile to run out of room on their networks to steal customers, and then charge their remaining customers similar or even higher prices to what they do now, to preserve margins.

The difficult with that argument is that if consumers are self-sorting in that way, it's actually a negative for Comcast, Altice (ATUS) and other cable companies. Cable companies usually charge the same price to their data-sipping subscribers as they do to their bingers. Officially, there’s a data cap and an overage charge; unofficially, promotional activity centered around “unlimited data included” is very common. That sort of self-sorting means that Verizon and T-Mobile are eating more subscribers and more monthly fees out of Comcast than they would be if FWA subscribers were a more heterogeneous collection of data consumers.

The Future's Winding Road

The real price point going forward for Comcast internet is almost certainly somewhere between its current level and the wireless giants. Management’s determination that they will not cut price doesn’t seem sustainable to me in the long run; going all the way down to the FWA level doesn’t seem necessary.

There are other potential headwinds. Comcast’s protests notwithstanding, last year it finally started offering a substantially cheaper Internet option for value-oriented consumers, the so-called “NOW internet” package. This is priced from $45 to as low as $30 depending on speed and has no data cap. Lower speeds mean it is harder to run over the cap in any event, but it shows that FWA’s margin pressure is very real. I won’t rerun all the math for a broad cut to $30 on the price; obviously, that would be catastrophic, but Comcast has done a reasonably good job so far of segmenting this offer.

Still, you can see now why Comcast’s multiple is so far below the norm. Investors simply don’t know right now what to pencil in for a long-run profit number; the only thing they really know is that management’s assurances that FWA was a minnow that couldn’t take meaningful market share has not borne out. If FWA growth really does taper out quickly and allow Comcast to escape margin pressure, the stock is a screaming bargain; if Comcast ends up having to significantly reprice the base, there could be real problems.

The one thing we haven’t talked about yet is possible upside from mobile, which is now Comcast’s fastest-growing division by consumer count. The issue here is that Comcast continues to refuse to break out this division separately in the numbers. It’s not that I don’t want to talk about it, it’s that I have no numbers to work with. We do know that it generated $3.7 billion in revenue last year, but that number comes without any cost disclosures. We do know it includes devices sales as well as service, and device sales have relatively little margin on them.

Investment Summary

The future of Comcast’s broadband business will be determined by T-Mobile and Verizon’s capacity numbers, not any numbers in Comcast’s earnings reports. It all comes down to just how many customers they are able to take from traditional cable. And what price it will take to stop them. Unease about what that number might be is almost certainly the primary driver of Comcast’s low stock multiple.

I would be surprised if Comcast fell too much further from here, as this company almost certainly has some level of future profit that can safely be relied upon. Even if the wireless pressure is enough to lower future profits to fair value level, it seems unlikely that Comcast is substantially overvalued at these levels.

I set a higher bar for my stocks than the same return I could get from an index fund, however. And I’m not sure if the upside here is as high as the bulls think; I see a larger role for wireless broadband in the future than Comcast management does. And then there is the small, but not meaningless, possibility of crashing through the floor if wireless really outperforms, or if the linear TV ad market does reset after all.

Despite the attractive P/E multiple, I’m going to continue to Avoid for now.

data.title: A Cautionary Tale
data.description:
I always invest with “caution” in mind. Over my 50 years on Wall Street, I have seen wild swings in both directions, and I am mindful of their impact on people’s portfolios. Last Friday, we all watched the equity markets get hit, with the DJIA down -748.63 points. During the last month, according to Bloomberg data, we have witnessed the DJIA -1.69%, the S&P 500 -1.71% and the NASDAQ -2.20%. These are meaningful numbers, especially if you are playing for “appreciation,” which I am currently not doing.

What I have been doing is investing for “income.” As of February 23, 2025, I have nine funds that I am using, that all pay over 20% in dividends. These funds, I point out, are all listed, trade on various exchanges, are public funds and have decent liquidity, in my opinion. Also, all of these funds pay their dividends monthly, meaning you can use the money for living, each month, or use the money to re-invest. In the tax-exempt funds that I use, I have 11 municipal bond funds, all paying monthly, and all have a yield of over 7.00%. Taxable or tax-exempt, I am investing for income.

“If you wish to succeed in life, make perseverance your bosom friend, experience your wise counselor, caution your elder brother, and hope your guardian genius.”

- Joseph Addison

Dividends, as many of you know, are based upon the principal value and not the liquidating value. Consequently, if a fund does not change its dividend, and you re-invested in it last month, your income will be higher from that fund in the new month. This is known as compound interest, and it is a quite valuable tool, in my opinion.

Right now, in my view, the markets are fractured. We have a new President and a host of new pronouncements, executive orders, tariffs and legislation that are causing great uncertainty for the markets, in my view. The bond markets have performed somewhat better, but with the 10-year Treasury yield at 4.43%, we are barely beating our inflation numbers, which do not seem to be dropping anytime soon as the Fed has informed us.

“Never throw caution to the wind. It could whip back into your eyes and blind you.”

- Stephen Colbert

Another note of caution is our pending trade war, which seems to draw nearer each day. The U.S. has imposed 25% tariffs on all imports of steel and aluminum, including supplies from allies, and announced a broader initiative to impose "reciprocal tariffs" across the board. This comes after a discussion about new tariffs for both Canada and Mexico, which I see as a negotiating point, but it is definitely worth some consideration for your investments. While I see President Trump's pronouncements as “forays,” I also recognize the serious implications that could result from them. What is clear is that the old way of doing business has vanished, and President Trump's new policy statements are a significant break from the past. We have entered a new era, and it is one more reason to invest with caution, as our future outcomes are quite uncertain.

“Caution, though very often wasted, is a good risk to take.”

- Josh Billings

Proceed with caution and common sense. That is my mantra for today’s markets.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SoFi Technologies: CEO's Upgraded Guidance Supercharges My Confidence
data.description:
SoFi's (NASDAQ:SOFI) share price has been weak after the release of 4Q24 results on 27 Jan 2025. Share price had fallen almost 18% in two weeks until a recent rebound late last week. While 4Q24 results delivered a double beat in revenue and EPS, I believe the sharp decline in share price reflects investors' disappointment in management's 2025 EPS guidance and skepticism about the validity of SoFi's long-term EPS guidance for 2026 where management communicated back in 4Q23 and reiterated in the 4Q24 results conference call.

I was also disappointed by the 2025 guidance and worried about whether the 2026 guidance is still relevant, but CEO Anthony Noto's latest Q&A during BofA 2025 Financial Services Conference seems subtly raising the guidance for 2026. His 20-30% ROE target and over 50% revenue contribution from fee-based businesses reinforce my confidence in SoFi of being a financial technology company rather than a traditional bank.

I rate Strong Buy on SoFi. My base case price target is set at $19 based on 25x P/E and an EPS estimate of $0.76/sh in 2026. If things turn out to be stronger than my estimate, share price could head towards my blue-sky price target of $23 based on 30x FY26 P/E. I believe SoFi has huge growth potential and the recent share price weakness offers an excellent buying opportunity.

Let's take a look at SoFi's 4Q24 results & 2025 Guidance

CEO Anthony Noto stated in 4Q24 results press release that "2025 was SoFi's best year ever". He is indeed correct if you look at SoFi's double beat 4Q24 results. Adjusted revenue grew 24% yoy to $739m, beating consensus estimate of 11% growth. Adjusted EPS reached $0.05 taking full year adjusted EPS to $0.15 which was 30% ahead of management guidance of $0.11-0.12 during 3Q24 results. Therefore, it is hard to believe that a rational investor would dump SoFi' shares on the back of this amazing results.

So, what triggered the share price collapse on 27 Jan? The likely culprit is SoFi's conservative EPS guidance for 2025. Besides guiding revenue to grow 23-26%, management expect EPS to be $0.25-0.27 in 2025, representing 73% yoy growth based on the mid-point guided EPS range. This might not seem concerning at first glance. However, recall that management had projected an EPS of $0.55-0.80 in 2026 during 4Q23 earnings call and this is up to 3x higher than the 2025 guidance. This big contrast likely fueled investor skepticism and contributed to the sharp decline in share price after results.

Is the concern overdone? Management has a Strong Track Record in Beating its Guidance

SoFi's management is good at managing investors' expectation. In 2024, management has raised its earnings guidance twice. Compared the actual adjusted diluted EPS to the original guidance, actual EPS was double.

Looking at Earnings Surprise data collected by Seeking Alpha, there was only one earnings miss out of the last 12 quarters. This suggests that management has a strong tendency to give conservative guidance. Therefore, it is not unreasonable to assume the $0.27 adjusted diluted EPS for 2025 could have been under-guided.

Anthony Noto has subtly raised guidance for 2026 during BofA conference

While the management team reiterating the old guidance for 2026 during the 4Q23 earnings call, CEO Anthony Noto gave me a big surprise when he talked about the growth outlook for SoFi during BofA 2025 Financial Services Conference on 11 Feb 2025. On revenue, instead of 20-25% CAGR growth, he said, "We believe the opportunities for growth are so significant that we just raised our 2023 to 2026 revenue CAGR to more than 25%."

More importantly, he said, "We have more confidence than ever in our 2026 EPS guidance of $0.55 to $0.80 per share". Compared to investors' skepticism, this is such a big contrast.

In addition, he said, "we believe we can drive 20% to 30% return on equity as the business continues to scale and matures". On margins, he said, "we believe our EBITDA margins long term are 30%, and our GAAP net income margins long term are 20%."

By using the new guidance, I estimate SoFi's EPS in 2026 to be $0.76. This is close to the high bar of the guidance range of $0.8.

SoFi is Not a Traditional Bank

Technology Platform + Financial Service will soon take up half of the business

SoFi has three business segments, namely Lending, Technology Platform and Financial Service. Compared their growth in 2024, Technology Platform + Financial Service net revenue growth was superior at 54% vs 8% in traditional lending business. More importantly, Technology Platform + Financial Service revenue already accounted for 47%, a big jump from 38% in 2023.

Fee-based revenue will eventually take up >50% of total revenue

Another thing making SoFi less like a traditional bank is the surging fee-based revenue. SoFi made a total of $970m fee-based revenue in 2024 which grew at skyrocket speed of 75%. This also represented 34% of total revenue. Compared to lending business, fee-based business requires less capital and hence less exposure to credit risk but generating higher ROE. CEO Anthony Noto said, "You can expect that our fee-based businesses and revenue that continues to increase well over 50% of our total revenue". I have never seen a traditional bank having over 50% of its revenue coming from fee-based business. This could be another evidence suggesting SoFi is not a traditional bank.

20-30% ROE is not a traditional bank that can achieve

SoFi's ROE was 8.5% in 2024. However, CEO Anthony Noto aims to bring SoFi's ROE to 20-30% in the long term by leveraging technology and fee-based revenue model. With my earlier net income estimate, SoFi's ROE could reach 13% in 2026. The below chart shows that Wall Street banks had ROE ranging from 6 to 17% in 2024. If we look back since 2015, the highest recorded ROE was 22% by Goldman Sachs in 3Q21 and higher than 20% ROE only happened once in the past decade. This suggests that a 20-30% ROE is well above the norm for traditional banks.

Valuation: SoFi should be valued as a high growth Fintech

While SoFi is a bank holding company with a charter, its business is becoming less like a traditional bank. If SoFi could sustain a 20-30% ROE in the long term, there is no reason to benchmark SoFi to the lower ROE Wall Street banks. In reality, it is more reasonable to benchmark SoFi to high growth Fintech companies, where they could trade up to 40x P/E.

For conservative purpose, I believe 20-30x P/E could be a target multiple range for SoFi. Based on my EPS estimate of $0.76 in 2026 and 25x P/E, I arrive at a price target of $19. If under the blue-sky scenario, my target price could be raised to $23 based on 30x P/E.

Downside Risks to my Buy Recommendation

If SoFi's earnings don't exceed management guidance in 2025, it might trigger another round of sell off as this casts doubts on the long-term $0.55-0.8 EPS guidance in 2026.

A ROE of 20-30% is a key matrix differentiating SoFi from other traditional banks. Hence, its ROE improvement would be closely monitored by investors going forward. Any signs of ROE improvement slow down or even decline could lead to negative sentiment on the stock.

Conclusion

My buy recommendation on SoFi is based on my belief that the market has mistakenly sold off the stock without much considering management's track record in guidance and investors' expectation management. I believe the latest EPS guidance of $0.25-0.27 for 2025 is way too conservative vs my estimate of $0.4. If SoFi continues to beat consensus in 1Q25 and 2Q25, I believe the market should regain confidence in its 2026 EPS guidance of $0.55-0.8. In addition, CEO Anthony Noto's long-term ROE target of 20-30% could also be a key re-rating factor as the market sees improving ROE and start to believe SoFi is not a traditional bank but more of a high growth financial technology company.

data.title: CBRE Group: Resilience Through Adversity, The Worst Is Behind
data.description:
With a market capitalization of over $40 billion, CBRE Group (NYSE:CBRE) is the global leader in commercial real estate services, investment and development, as well as the largest company in the sector by revenue.

After weathering the sharp decline in commercial real estate activity in recent years, management is now seeking to lead the sector's recovery by capitalizing on secular changes within the market. The return of work to the office, which has once again boosted demand for this type of space and the arrival of new projects in the area of ​​real estate for data centers, have contributed to a revitalization of the sector, which is already beginning to be reflected in the company's accounts after the presentation of quarterly and annual results last week.

I believe that these market movements have contributed to creating new tailwinds for CBRE, which enters 2025 with a solid financial position and excellent prospects for growth and expansion, which could renew the bullish potential of its shares this year. This, accompanied by an environment of robust economic growth in the US economy and the expectation that the Fed will soon resume interest rate cuts, which is why I begin my coverage of CBRE with a solid Buy rating.

Towards A More Resilient Model

The so-called 'resilient' business lines (comprising facilities management, property management, project management, loan servicing, valuation and recurring investment management fees), more resistant to changes in the economic cycle, achieved a revenue growth of 16% year-over-year (y/y) during the last quarter of 2024, and a 14% for the whole past year, driven by global property sales revenue (+35% y/y), and mortgage origination revenue (+37% y/y) increase in the last three months, the latter with a strong performance in the US market.

In this regard, management set to carry out a reorganization of its main operating segments, seeking to benefit from secular tailwinds in the commercial real estate market. The recent growth in demand for office space, driven by the return to office trend decreed by many companies, combined with an increase in interest in other types of projects, such as Data Centers, are generating new opportunities within the market for the company.

During the earnings call, CBRE CEO, Bob Sulentic, noted that the company is receiving a lot of interest in this regard, which has led to a significant increase in the data center sector's share of earnings (emphasis in bold by the author):

I'll conclude with some thoughts on our participation in the data center sector for two reasons: first, we're receiving many questions on this topic given the amount of activity in the sector; and second, our work with data centers clearly demonstrates how our strategy plays out in practice. A foundational element of that strategy is to focus financial and operational resources in areas benefiting from secular tailwinds. And we have brought this focus to the data center sector, growing its contribution to core EBITDA from 3% three years ago to almost 10% in 2024. Over that time, our total data center profit has increased over 2.5 times.

According to the last company's results presentation, the Property Management business will be spun off from the Advisory segment to form a new segment called 'Building Operations & Experience' (replacing Global Workplace Solutions) which will house the results of 'Industrious', a company dedicated to flexible office spaces recently fully acquired by CBRE. This, after Property Management showed solid performance over the past year, with year-over-year growth of 15% in 2024, reaching $2,123 million dollars in net revenue, also led by the US market as the recent partnership with Brookfield office portfolio started to give results.

In turn, Project Management will now report as a separate operating segment from the former GWS, where the Turner & Townsend business will be located after CBRE increased its stake on the company to 70%, seeking to capitalize on a growing market demand for infrastructure, energy and data center spaces, which, I believe, will be one of the main focuses that management will look to emphasize in the coming years. Meanwhile, Real Estate Investments segment, will not undergo any structural changes in its organization.

New Growth Catalysts

I think the company is looking to capture two major movements in the market now: the first, linked to the revitalization of demand for office space, and the second, linked to the growing interest in investing in data centers and industrial spaces.

Office rental growth continued to strengthen in the latter part of the year, after showing a stronger-than-expected increase during the third quarter of last year. Particularly in the US market, where they increased by 28% y/y during Q4 2024, extending an outstanding 26% increase to its record high during Q3. The latter being led by a strong performance in markets such as New York, San Francisco and Dallas, as highlighted by the company in the earnings call, reflecting a positive impact on the mortgage origination business in the last quarter, which consolidated a remarkable growth of 37% in the last year.

On the other hand, the Building Operations & Experience and Project Management segments will be at the forefront of large infrastructure and Data Center projects after having incorporated Turner & Townsend, which recorded a 20% increase in revenue in this last quarter in annual terms.

In my opinion, I see this as a good move by the company, which is looking to optimize its earnings mix for the coming years to take advantage of two major growth drivers with enormous momentum in recent quarters.

Another point I would like to highlight as a key driver for the sector is the macroeconomic context, with a US economy growing at a solid pace, a persistently tight labor market and the expectation that the Fed will cut interest rates again, at least 1 or 2 more times this year, which, I think, has renewed the market's hopes that the commercial real estate business is finally picking up again, after having been hit hard in recent years by high interest rates, inflation and the pandemic. Personally, I am very bullish on the sector for this year.

Firepower To Keep Growing

Another thing last week's earnings presentation demonstrated us was that the company's ability to continue channeling capital into new projects is at an all-time high as CBRE achieved its best quarter in history in terms of earnings per share and free cash flow, giving the company plenty of room to continue expanding its product portfolio.

CBRE's Operating Cash Flow exceeded $1.7 billion, translating into a record annual Free Cash Flow (FCF) for the company of more than $1.5 billion in 2024, up from $202 million in 2023, with the final quarter of the year showing an impressive FCF of $1.377 billion, an all-time high record also for a single quarter for the company. Meanwhile, free cash flow conversion, which measures a company's ability to convert its operating earnings into FCF over a given period, reached nearly 100%, comfortably exceeding management's target range of between 75% and 85% for 2024.

Another strong point to highlight was the significant improvement in margins in the company's main operating segments, particularly in the last quarter, which accompanied the growth in operating income, for both main sectors, Advisory and GWS. This also comes as a result of a cost reduction program announced by the company at the beginning of 2024 that evidently yielded very good results for GWS, achieving its best net income performance in at least the last five years, while in the case of Advisory, the operating margin achieved its best record since the last quarter of 2021.

On the other hand, the company has demonstrated a prudent management of debt, with net leverage closing the year at 0.93 and showing a favorable progression in recent quarters after a notable drop from 1.58 in the second quarter of 2024, while net debt, although stable in the latter part of the year, showed a reduction compared to the first half of last year to 2.5 billion dollars. Finally, CBRE's liquidity levels, measured as the company's immediate cash availability, recovered to its highest level since the end of 2023.

Management projects Core EPS for this year of between $5.80 and $6.10, representing growth of around 16% compared to $5.10 per share in 2024, with operating income supported by the good outlook for resilient businesses and continued momentum in leasing.

Risks

Even though the current macroeconomic situation is favorable to its core business, CBRE is not exempt from risks linked to political and regulatory factors that could seriously affect the company's activities. A new surge in inflation in the United States, which leads the Federal Reserve to keep rates high for longer or even raise them again, could also affect CBRE, both in its income and in the valuation of its assets.

On the other hand, its high geographic diversification, while it can be seen as a positive aspect, also entails exchange rate risks, for example, in the face of sudden changes in the exchange rates of the different currencies of the countries in which CBRE operates, which could erode the company's results in cases where the US dollar appreciates strongly against other currencies. In 2024, approximately 44% of revenues came from outside the United States.

Another aspect to highlight within the risks is the one related to its current price. After CBRE stock has risen by almost 60% over the past year, investors may decide to take profits and see the stock fall, and while this could be seen as a risk, it may also represent an opportunity to buy the dip as long as the company's fundamentals remain strong, since it will be a matter of time before the stock recovers its value and resumes its upward path. However, I am not seeing a reason for the bullish rally to be interrupted at this time, except for material changes in the company's business or a very strong general fall in the stock market, a risk that is equally inherent to any other stock.

Valuation

Traditional valuation metrics for CBRE are mixed and vary depending on which parameters are taken. According to the Fwd P/E, which discounts future earnings and is one of the most followed indicators by investors, the shares are 32% below the industry. However, the shares seem to be trading at fair value considering multiples such as the EV to EBIT ratio, and even somewhat expensive considering EV/EBITDA ratio.

If we look at the midpoint of the company's earnings per share projections of between $5.8 and $6.1 for this year and apply the forward P/E, we get a value per share of around $135, which is pretty in line with current value. However, I'm going to give it a handicap as I think the company's growth drivers and fundamentals widely support more share price momentum, although I would not rule out that prices may see a correction in the coming months before regaining the upward trend to new highs again. I tend to think that this could be happening as some investors have taken profits after a good year and the market's decline in recent sessions. At the same time, others could be waiting to see the company's next results, which is why the stock has been doing poorly over the last month.

The consensus of Wall Street analysts currently gives CBRE shares an upside potential of almost 13% for the next year, to an average of $154, while recommendations are widely distributed between Buy and Strong Buy.

Conclusion

The strength of CBRE's business and future prospects makes me feel optimistic about the company's share price performance in the coming months, as a prudent and disciplined financial management, together with efficient capital deployment, has allowed CBRE to weather the decline in the sector activity in recent years, having solid support now to undertake new projects to boost operating profits.

However, we must bear in mind that the business may be vulnerable to an unstable macroeconomic and political environment, which may bring complications in the short term. This aside, the outlook remains excellent for the market and for the leading company in it.

data.title: Bullish On The Trade Desk: My Case For Upgrading To Buy
data.description:
I downgraded The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD) to a Hold on November 9, 2024. I reaffirmed that rating on December 12, 2024, primarily because its valuation reached unsustainable levels without enormous upside surprises to analysts' revenue and earnings estimates. I said the following in my December 2024 article:

Initiatives like Ventura [a streaming TV operating system] are a long-term opportunity. If the initiative pays off, it may take three years or more to benefit the company and investors. There are not enough near-term catalysts like the Olympics or elections to justify pushing the stock higher, increasing the likelihood that the stock will be a dud in 2025 compared to other investment choices.

That forecast looks accurate after investors perused The Trade Desk's fourth-quarter earnings release and listened to the earnings call. After it recorded revenue that missed analysts' estimates, investors worried about whether competition was eating away at the company's market share or something was wrong with the business. Management acknowledged it was having several execution issues. Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Jeff Green described those execution issues in the following way on the fourth quarter 2024 earnings:

To, again compare it to a pro sports team, we have a championship team. We've proven that for the last eight years as a public company, but we turned over the ball too many times in this game, and that's why we lost the game. The opportunity is not smaller than we thought. In fact, it's the opposite. My focus the last quarter and now is about recalibrating the company to become a bigger company because we are facing a bigger opportunity faster than we thought.

The company also provided revenue guidance for the first quarter below analysts' estimates, making its near-term prospects bleak. However, The Trade Desk's long-term revenue growth prospects still look solid.

This article will discuss how Alphabet's (GOOGL)(GOOG) Google's apparent retreat from its network business could help rejuvenate The Trade Desk's revenue growth. It will discuss the fourth quarter 2024 results, valuation, risks, and why I am upgrading the stock to a buy.

The Trade Desk could benefit from Google's potential exit from the Open Internet

Google is the target of multiple antitrust lawsuits in the United States, alleging that it has illegally monopolized online search and advertising. It has already lost one case in August 2024. Additionally, on February 21, the European Union hit it with fresh accusations of violating big tech rules. This increased global regulatory scrutiny will likely force Google to "play more fairly" in advertising on the open internet.

The open internet is a term coined by CEO Green to refer to the network of websites, apps, and other digital environments accessible to all companies and not controlled by a single entity. Google's Network segment is a collection of ad platforms consisting of Google Display Network, AdMob, AdSense, and DV360 (Display & Video 360) that compete on the open internet against The Trade Desk's Demand Side Platform ("DSP") and others.

CEO Green said the following about Google's potential retreat from the open internet on the fourth quarter earnings call:

We are preparing for a world where Google exits the open internet. I'm confident that one way or another Google will exit the open internet. I think they should. Most of their antitrust and regulatory problems come from the draconian ways they have engaged with the open internet in the past. In April of 2024, Facebook shut down their news program thereby distancing itself further from one of the most important pillars of the open internet. Some evidence suggests the substantial majority of spend going through DV360 is routed to the Google Owned and Operated platform of YouTube. Regardless of what happens with the pending trial decision, Google will likely distance itself from the Open Internet. If and when Google exits the Open Internet, they will leave a big hole and a big opportunity for the rest of us.

In other words, Jeff Green believes that Google will de-emphasize the open internet and instead favor routing advertisers to its proprietary internet properties, such as YouTube. Jeff Green often refers to the proprietary internet properties of large internet players like Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), and Google as "walled gardens." Green believes these walled gardens lack objectivity. For instance, on the fourth quarter earnings call, he says the following about Amazon and objectivity:

We will promote and protect our objectivity more than ever. More and more the only competitors we encounter today have the worst objectivity problems. Amazon is asking advertisers big and small for their advertising budgets. Meanwhile, Amazon competes with most of the fortune 500 companies in some way, whether we are talking about Microsoft (MSFT) in cloud, or P&G (PG) in CPG [consumer packaged goods] products, or UPS (UPS), or Nike (NKE), or all the rest. In our very first business plan 15 years ago we argued that the objective, independent DSP should get the lion's share of the marketplace. They'd be the only company that can be trusted.

Jeff Green believes that antitrust concerns will eventually force all the large technology companies off the open internet or substantially reduce their role. If and when that happens, he thinks The Trade Desk can fill the large void created after the large players step back into their walled gardens. Additionally, he believes the open internet can drive higher returns on investment for advertisers and will become increasingly more attractive than advertising on YouTube or Facebook over time.

Investors should monitor the antitrust lawsuits against large tech companies with advertising as part of the business model and note events like Facebook's closing of its news program because these actions benefit The Trade Desk in the long term. The walled gardens' potential retreat from the open internet won't happen overnight; it's likely to be more gradual. This secular tailwind could be a long-term growth driver over the next five to ten years, not a near-term catalyst.

Company Fundamentals

Annual 2024 Gross spending on The Trade Desk's Platform exceeded $12 billion, the company's highest total in history. The company believes this number is evidence of continued market share gains. CEO Green said on the earnings call, "We continued to significantly outpace the broader digital advertising market."

The company's fourth-quarter 2024 revenue increased 22% over the previous year's comparable quarter to $741 million, missing analysts' estimates by -$18.55 million. Its 2024 annual revenue increased approximately 26% to $2.44 billion.

During the fourth quarter, the video segment, including CTV, had a high-40s percentage of spending on the ad platform and continued growing faster than total revenue. Mobile had a mid-30s percentage of spending during the quarter. Display ads comprised a low-double-digit percentage on the platform, and Audio was 5%.

North America accounted for 88% of spending for the fourth quarter, and International represented about 12%. International business grew much faster than North America for the eighth straight quarter—a positive secular trend. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Laura Schenkein said on the fourth quarter 2024 earnings call, "CTV growth across international regions was particularly strong during the fourth quarter and throughout 2024."

The Trade Desk's fourth quarter 2024 GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) net income increased 87% to $182 million. Its GAAP diluted earnings-per-share ("EPS") increased 89% to $0.36, beating analysts' estimates by $0.03. Fourth quarter 2024 non-GAAP adjusted net income of $297 million. Its non-GAAP fully diluted EPS was $0.59, beating analysts' estimates by $0.02.

Its 2024 annual net income increased by 120% to $393 million, and its annual 2024 GAAP diluted EPS increased 117% to $0.80.

The Trade Desk's fourth quarter 2024 adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) increased 23% over the previous year's comparable quarter to $350 million. Fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA margin remained flat year over year at 47%. Management was disappointed in these quarterly numbers.

Full-year 2024 annual adjusted EBITDA increased 31% to $1.01 billion. The full-year adjusted EBITDA margin was approximately 41%.

CFO Laura Schenkein said on the earnings call:

For the first time, in our 8.5 years as a public company, excluding the first quarter of 2020, our results came in below our expectations. As a company, we take great pride in our ability to forecast accurately, and we take full ownership of this shortfall. Importantly, this miss was not due to lack of opportunity or increased competition, it was on us. We are implementing the strategic changes Jeff outlined in our business and I believe that will give us an opportunity to continue delivering strong revenue growth throughout this year and beyond.

The CFO's language can shake investor confidence by raising questions about the company's internal execution and forecasting accuracy. This management team doesn't often miss its guidance, so the market still gives it some benefit of the doubt. Still, there is more downside in the stock price if the company misses on its guidance in future quarters.

The company's most significant expense in reconciling net income and adjusted EBITDA was stock-based compensation ("SBC"). The Trade Desk's quarterly and annual SBC-to-revenue were 17.44% and 20.33%, respectively. The following table shows that The Trade Desk's SBC-to-revenue ratio is on the high side compared to a few advertising and marketing peers.

Company name Quarterly SBC-to-revenue Annual SBC-to-revenue
Zeta Global 17.58% 33.33%
The Trade Desk 17.44% 20.33%
PubMatic 13.17% 10.81%
Magnite 11.52% 11.72%
Alphabet 6.02% 6.51%

Trailing 12-month ("TTM") cash from operation ("CFO") to sales was 30.25%. TTM CFO was $739.46 million, and TTM FCF was $632.39 million.

The Trade Desk's cash and short-term investments were $1.921 billion, with no long-term debt.

Under an existing authorization plan, the company repurchased $57 million of its shares during the fourth quarter. Management announced an additional authorization under the share repurchase program, raising the total to $1 billion, including the amount remaining from the existing authorization. CFO Schenkein said, "Given our strong balance sheet and consistent cash flow generation, we plan to continue opportunistic share repurchases, while also offsetting dilution from employee stock issuances."

Management's first-quarter revenue guidance of $575 was below analysts' estimates before earnings of $582.99. If The Trade Desk hits management's forecasts, revenue will grow 17% year over year. Suppose it hit analysts' estimates before earnings; revenue growth would be 18.7%. Post earnings, analysts revised their revenue estimates in line with management's forecasts to $575.28. Last year, the company grew its first-quarter 2024 revenue by 28.3%. So, the first quarter revenue forecasts represent a significant revenue growth deceleration from last year. On the earnings call, KeyBanc analyst Justin Patterson asked about "the company's potential to sustain a 20% plus compound growth rate over the next several years?" CEO Green only indirectly answered this question. He said (emphasis added):

We believe that while our share has been growing faster than any of our scaled competitors, I also believe the opportunity is growing, too, and that's why we are recalibrating now. I believe that we can reaccelerate our growth again.

Management's first-quarter adjusted EBITDA guidance of approximately $145 million is 10.5% below the first-quarter 2024 adjusted EBITDA. If the company hits its first-quarter revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance, adjusted EBITDA margin will shrink eight points to 25%. Slower revenue growth and shrinking profitability failed to excite the market, causing investors to revalue the stock.

Valuation

The Trade Desk's price-to-sales (P/S) is 14.8, well below its three-, five-, and seven-year median, suggesting the market undervalues the stock. However, investors must remember that if sales growth continues to slow or profitability margins continue to shrink, the current valuation may look appropriate in hindsight.

The following table shows that the market values The Trade Desk at a 2025 forward P/S to estimated 2025 revenue growth of 0.70, a premium valuation to several of its peers in ad technology. The market has likely awarded it a premium valuation due to having the highest estimated 2025 revenue growth, TTM FCF margin, and second-highest TTM profit margin behind Alphabet. The Trade Desk will likely retain its current valuation if the market thinks it can maintain its estimated revenue growth and profitability margins. However, if the market believes the opposite, its valuation could shrink. For instance, if its revenue growth begins tailing off into the low double-digits, the market may begin valuing The Trade Desk similar to Alphabet or even below Alphabet.

Company 2025 forward P/S Analysts estimated 2025 forward revenue growth rates 2025 forward P/S to estimated 2025 revenue growth TTM FCF margin TTM Profit margin
Zeta Global Holdings (ZETA) 4.40 22.80% 0.19 8.74% -13.34
The Trade Desk 12.91 18.44% 0.70 25.86% 16.08%
Magnite (MGNI) 3.81 10.31% 0.37 23.73% 2.62%
PubMatic (PUBM) 2.23 7.65% 0.29 15.70% 5.96%
Alphabet 5.77 11.43% 0.50 20.78% 28.60%

Before fourth-quarter earnings, analysts estimated The Trade Desk's 2025 EPS at $1.96, which represents an 18.07% year-over-year EPS growth rate. At the February 21, 2024 closing price of $72.06, the stock would have had a forward 2025 P/E of 36.76 and a forward 2025 PEG ratio of 2.03 if analysts had not revised the 2025 estimated EPS growth rates down. I performed this calculation to illustrate what happens when analysts revise estimates.

After earnings, analysts revised EPS estimates for 2025 down to $1.80, representing 8.44% year-over-year EPS growth. As a result, the current forward 2025 Price/Earnings-to-growth ("PEG") ratio is 4.98 (2025 forward P/E of 42.07 divided by estimated EPS growth of 8.44%). Usually, growth investors will allow a PEG ratio to reach 2.0 before considering a stock overvalued. Suppose the stock sold at a 2025 PEG ratio of 2.0; the stock price would be $30.38, a 58% decline from the February 21 closing stock price of $72.06.

The 2026 forward PEG ratio is 1.52. If the stock traded at a 2026 PEG ratio of 2.0, the stock price would be $98.91, a 37% rise from the February 21 closing stock price. What this tells me is that there is an enormous amount of pressure for The Trade Desk's stock price to go lower in 2025, especially if the company misses analysts' estimates and Wall Street revises 2025 EPS estimates down again. However, analysts expect EPS growth to re-accelerate in 2026, justifying a higher stock price in 2026.

The market often considers a stock overvalued when its FCF yield is low. Generally, investors should be cautious when a growth stock's FCF yield goes below 1.0% because it's challenging for a company to grow FCF fast enough for its stock price to continue going up or maintain the same level. For the last half of 2024, The Trade Desk FCF yield dove below 1.0% (an FCF yield of 0.77% at the time of my December 12, 2024 article). At the same time, TTM FCF declined by 13.64% year over year in the third quarter of 2024, putting pressure on the stock to decline. Investors should not have been surprised by the stock's pullback after the fourth quarter of 2024 earnings.

As of the February 22, 2024 closing price, the FCF yield is 1.75%. Although the FCF yield may not be juicy enough for a value investor, some risk-tolerant growth investors may be interested in The Trade Desk at the current valuation.

Risks

CEO Green believes that Google and other walled gardens will leave or reduce their business on the open internet, but that is only an opinion. If Google, Amazon, and Meta decide to compete more ferociously in the open market, that competition could reduce The Trade Desk's revenue growth or margins just enough to make the stock unattractive to investors. Despite Green's prediction, Marketing Charts estimated that collectively, Google, Facebook, and Amazon controlled 62.3% of the digital ad market in 2021, 60.8% at the end of 2024, and forecasts 62.1% in 2026. The most significant difference between 2021 and 2026 is that Google and Meta lost market share, and Amazon gained the market share lost by the top two players. So, some believe the big three, Google, Meta, and Amazon, will remain a substantial force in digital advertising, whether on the open internet or safely ensconced inside the walled gardens.

The Trade Desk also faces significant competition aside from the walled gardens from Criteo's (CRTO) Commerce Max DSP, MediaMath, Yahoo's DSP, Adform, CitrusAd, and SiteScout. Additionally, marketing technology company Zeta Global, which I recently wrote about, has a highly competitive offering for advertisers.

Lastly, investors didn't like hearing that the company underperformed this quarter due to execution issues. They will watch closely to see if the Trade Desk continues to fumble the ball. The company missing management's revenue and profitability guidance in future quarters could adversely impact the stock's P/S and P/E valuations directly and indirectly negatively impact valuations based on FCF. The market would likely respond by driving the stock price even lower.

Upgrading The Trade Desk to a buy

Since few near-term catalysts exist, don't expect much excitement from the company this year. Momentum investors or people looking for quick short-term returns should avoid this stock. Alternatively, the long-term thesis for investing in this company still exists.

CFO Laura Schenkein said on the fourth quarter earnings call:

When I look across our list of growth drivers, most of them are still in their early stages compared to where we expect them to be in the next 5 years to 10 years. CTV advertising remains a small fraction of total TV ad spend relative to linear. Retail media is scaling rapidly, evolving from an emerging trend into a core digital advertising channel as brands are recognizing its ability to drive both performance and measurement. And in most global markets, decisioned programmatic is still in early stages of adoption, with tremendous long term growth potential.

CEO Jeff Green believes this company has ten sustainable secular tailwinds, which he discussed extensively in the third-quarter earnings call. This article discussed one of those secular trends (Google de-emphasizing its open internet business). Additionally, despite significant competition, the company has a massive market available for growth.

With annual revenue of $2.4 billion, The Trade Desk has only penetrated 0.25% of the open internet. So, the company has a long growth runway if it executes. The valuation has dropped to a level where long-term investors (five years or longer) can start repurchasing the stock. I raise The Trade Desk to a buy for aggressive growth investors.

data.title: Dollar General: Too Cheap To Ignore, A Turnaround In Sight
data.description:
Dollar General (NYSE:DG) stock has been in the doghouse for a while now, as it is over 70% off its all-time high from 2022. However, it looks like a worthwhile investment at current levels. The valuation is too cheap to ignore, and EPS is expected to rebound in Fiscal 2025. For context, DG's fiscal year generally concludes at the end of January or the beginning of February.

The investment thesis here is simple. Dollar General is a historically strong retailer (strong profitability, steady growth in EPS, revenue, and FCF) that has been facing headwinds in recent years, especially due to high inflation, which hurt its margins. As a result, the stock is trading well under its historical average valuation, and this suggests that there's upside potential, especially since growth is expected from here on out, and inflation isn't as bad as it was two or three years ago.

It's worth mentioning that I'm also not worried about tariff risks, as the company imports only 4% of its products from China.

There are execution risks because the company can fail to meet EPS expectations, as it has in the past. Moreover, there's a resistance level just above the current price that people should pay attention to. But I am comfortable with the risks at the current price. Thus, I rate the stock as a Buy.

A Historically Strong Business That Can Rebound

Some people who see DG stock under $77 right now may not realize how solid the company's track record is if you look past the recent decline in profitability. Right now, its ROE is 19.4%, which is still quite a high number (sector median is 10.7%), but this number has generally been higher -- even reaching 40% in Fiscal 2023. And up until recently, its net income and gross profit margins have been steady. See below.

It also has a good history of growing its earnings, revenue, and free cash flow, and this isn't the first time it has faced a downturn in EPS or FCF.

I'm aware that past performance doesn't predict future results, but I value a solid track record of profitability and growth because it shows that solid results are possible with this business as long as it gets back on the right track. I mean, I don't think the business is broken. It's a dollar store company, not a tech stock that got disrupted by some new, advanced technology.

Some factors that hurt the company were high inflation and cash-strapped consumers, but those things don't last forever, and annual inflation has already moderated quite a bit to 3% in January. In addition, Dollar General is actively working on ways to improve its business. For instance, in 2024, it set a goal to reduce net SKUs by 1,000, making the business more efficient. It's also exiting temporary warehouses and investing in new, permanent distribution centers.

Quote from DG's CEO during the Q3 earnings call:

Meanwhile, we've augmented our permanent DC footprint with two new facilities in Colorado and Arkansas, each of which is open and now shipping to stores. By the end of this year, we anticipate these efforts will result in year-over-year reductions in stem miles of approximately 4% or greater in both our fresh and traditional supply chains.

Dollar General also plans to remodel thousands of stores in Fiscal 2025 while opening up 575 new ones in the US and an additional 15 in Mexico, so a recovery is possible.

Trading At A Very Low Valuation

Down from its highs, DG stock trades at a very low valuation. Let's start with its forward P/E 12 months out, which sits at 12.93x. The chart below looks all the way back to June 2012, and as you can tell, DG stock is trading well below the average forward P/E of 18.14x.

Also, DG's TTM non-GAAP P/E ratio sits at 12.54x, notably lower than the stock's five-year average of 19.75x.

The stock looks cheap even when looking at its forward price/FCF ratio of 17.2x. Just by eyeballing the chart, you can tell that a more normalized level is 20-22x.

Given the fact that growth is expected for Dollar General after Fiscal 2024 concludes, the stock does truly seem undervalued. Analysts even expect near-double-digit growth two years from now. If the company starts growing its earnings again, I don't see why it wouldn't trade above a 15x P/E ratio, at least, and that's where the upside potential can come from.

Some Risks To Consider

Downward revisions: One of the main risks to consider is the fact that Dollar General has been disappointing analysts lately. You can see this by looking at the consensus EPS revision trend below. For instance, at this time last year, the company was expected to report $7.45 in EPS for Fiscal 2024. Now, this has fallen to $5.74.

Thus, it's entirely possible that the forward estimates look good for DG at the moment but will fail to play out and lead to more downward revisions, which can put more pressure on the stock.

The stock is near resistance: This isn't a business risk, but it's still worth looking at. To me, it looks like the stock is still in a downtrend, and resistance is nearby. That resistance level, which has also acted as support, is around $78, and the level has been tested many times (check the arrows I added to the chart). I believe that if the stock closes notably above that resistance area and holds, the downtrend could then be over. But at the moment, we're not out of the woods.

The Bottom Line On DG Stock

After a 70%+ drop from its all-time high, DG stock may now be ready to recover. The low valuation, solid fundamentals (even now, after the fundamentals have deteriorated a bit), and turnaround initiatives — like store remodels, reduced SKUs, and investments in new, permanent distribution centers — suggest that growth in EPS is possible.

Nevertheless, I'm aware that the stock is still in a downtrend and near a resistance, making me hesitant to take a large position. I'll be watching for either a pullback to add to my position (so I can at least get a greater margin of safety) or for a break of the downtrend.

I'm also aware that the company can potentially disappoint analysts again, leading to more downward revisions. But with the valuation being well under historical levels, I'm willing to take on these risks for potential gains.

data.title: Hims & Hers Declines - How The GLP-1 Market Shift Affects Growth
data.description:
Hims & Hers Health (NYSE:HIMS) stock plummeted 25% last Friday after the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) declared that Ozempic and Wegovy (semaglutide) are no longer in shortage. As a result, state-licensed pharmacies and outsourcing facilities must stop compounding these medications by April 22, 2025, and May 22, 2025, respectively. Through its state-licensed 503A compounding pharmacies and FDA-registered 503B outsourcing facility, Hims offered compounded versions of semaglutide, a popular GLP-1 receptor agonist, during the shortage. Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) semaglutide is a multi-billion-dollar weight loss and diabetes drug that undoubtedly boosted interest in Hims’ weight loss services (20,000 new subscribers from pre-existing customers in Q3 alone). Because weight loss is a chronic disease that requires extended treatment, many of Hims’ subscribers will soon have to obtain their semaglutide from Novo Nordisk. Weight loss is one of the four key services on Hims’ platform (the others are sexual health, dermatology, and mental health) and carries a “higher monthly average price” relative to the other services.

As the company explained during their Q3 earnings call:

We believe that with continued innovation and personalization for individual clinical needs, these benefits will continue to compound across specialties in the future. Monthly online revenue per average subscriber increased 24% in the third quarter relative to last year to $67. A continued shift to more premium personalized offerings has been one contributor towards this dynamic, which has offset headwinds from a migration toward longer duration commitments that carry a lower average monthly price.

The success of our weight-loss specialty has been another key driver in the increase in monthly online revenue per average subscriber through two mechanisms. Firstly, our weight-loss solutions carry a higher monthly average price relative to other product offerings. Historically, our offerings have ranged from $35 to $55 per month depending on specialty and duration.

The end of the shortage is no surprise to Hims. They plan to launch a generic version of Novo Nordisk’s Saxenda (liraglutide), another GLP-1 receptor agonist, later this year. However, liraglutide’s efficacy in weight reduction falls significantly short of semaglutide.

In this randomized clinical trial that included 338 participants, mean body weight change from baseline to 68 weeks was –15.8% with semaglutide vs –6.4% with liraglutide, a statistically significant difference.

As a result, Novo Nordisk has longed deprioritized Saxenda in favor of Wegovy.

Let’s not forget about Eli Lilly’s (LLY) tirzepatide (a GLP-1 and GIP receptor agonist), or Zepbound. The tirzepatide shortage ended in October. Prior to this, Hims offered compounded tirzepatide. In a recent study, tirzepatide proved superior to semaglutide in weight loss, delivering an average weight loss of 20.2%. So liraglutide is quite inferior to both semaglutide and tirzepatide. Hims could differentiate itself by offering liraglutide for a much lower price and as an oral (both tirzepatide and semaglutide are available only as injectables in obesity). Regardless, the end of the shortage for the popular weight loss drugs has a major impact on Hims’ business prospects. In Q3, Hims’ Y/Y subscriber growth was 44%. However, the company did note that “there was 40-plus percent year-over-year growth in our subscribers excluding the GLP-1 business.” The largest impact from the GLP-1 shortage resolution will likely surround Hims’ “monthly online revenue per average subscriber,” which grew 24% Y/Y from $54 in Q3 ’23 to $67 in Q3 ’24. As Hims likes to point out, its weight loss services are “sticky,” with “70% of patients continuing their subscription by 12 weeks.” So, the loss of a lot of their current GLP-1 clientele, coupled with a complete absence of semaglutide/tirzepatide compounds, should become apparent when Hims reports Q2 ’25 earnings later this year. While liraglutide is a sensible fallback, it is not likely to come close to replacing semaglutide and tirzepatide. Hims' providers could still prescribe Wegovy/Zepbound injectables. However, its margins, as a result of not providing the compounded versions themselves, will not be as robust and much of the value proposition associated with a Hims' weight loss subscription will be "lost in translation."

While these developments are a bummer for their weight loss services, all hope is not lost. Hims is riding tailwinds in the global telemedicine market, which is expected to grow at a CAGR of 19% and reach “$432.31 billion by 2032.” They have built a brand, particularly in sensitive areas like sexual health and hair loss, that is compelling and long-lasting.

Right now, I am most interested in whether a quarter slash in Hims’ valuation is justified. To do this, I “reverse-engineered” a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis (see my bio for model assumptions) to uncover the market’s implicit expectations of growth before and after last week’s valuation adjustment.

Let’s first look at Hims’ financial performance in the trailing twelve months (TTM).

Here are some expectations for 2025:

The Free Cash Flow and FCF margin are implied by Hims’ current market capitalization, projected revenue for 2025, and its P/FCF.

Now, the fun part: If I assume their FCF margin will remain ~13% during the next 9 years, I can adjust the projected Y/Y revenue growth until the model outputs an enterprise value (discounted cash flows + terminal value) that is equal to Hims’ current valuation. In doing so, I can speculate on the market’s implicit expectations for revenue growth.

If we return Hims’ EV to what it was before last week’s 25% haircut, expectations for Y/Y revenue growth were roughly 23.5%. So, the market appears to have lowered its expectations of Y/Y revenue growth by 4.5%. It doesn’t sound like much, but because of growth's compounding nature, slight adjustments can have large repercussions on valuation. Considering everything discussed above, I think this is a reasonable adjustment by market participants.

Financial Health

In Q3, Hims reported revenues of $401.6 million, representing 77% Y/Y growth. Cost of revenues was $83.7 million, or 20.84% of revenues. SG&A and R&D costs totaled $274.4 million and $21.1 million, respectively. Impressively, Hims’ SG&A costs only made up 68% of revenues in Q3. This figure was consistently above 80% just a few quarters back. Net income for Q3 was $75.6 million. Cash from operations was $85.3 million.

After deducting $3.3 in capital expenditure, Hims’ free cash flow was $82 million.

Turning to the balance sheet, as of September 2024, Hims had $165.5 million in cash and equivalents. Short-term investments totaled $88.6 million. Total current assets were $326.4 million, while total current liabilities were $152.6 million. As a result, Hims’ current ratio is roughly 2, which indicates good liquidity. The company is without long-term debt.

Hims will report Q4 earnings on Monday after the market’s close. The revenue estimate is $470.5 million. EPS GAAP is expected to come in at $0.11. Outside of the obvious (e.g., revenue, profitability, subscriber numbers), investors should be focused on management’s insights on how the shortage resolution will impact revenue streams and what strategies are in place to address it, such as the timeline for liraglutide’s rollout. I will be particularly interested in the company’s plan to attract and retain weight loss subscribers in the coming absence of popular GLP-1 compounds.

HIMS Stock: Balanced Take & Investment Recommendation

In conclusion, the GLP-1 shortage resolution is expected to have a material impact on Hims’ growth prospects. As this was not a surprise (both Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly have invested a lot of money into manufacturing capabilities), it is back to the basics for the company ("core"), including the addition of a substandard GLP-1 to an equation absent of heavy hitters. With or without the popular GLP-1 drugs, Hims is priced for considerable growth (that is now, coincidentally, even with its industry's projected CAGR per my DCF model), and it is likely achievable given its brand and the opportunity that is right in front of Hims. My DCF analysis reveals that Hims is reasonably priced, warranting a “hold” and "market perform" recommendation. Investors should be aware of some idiosyncratic risks, including, but not limited to, a larger than expected impact on Hims’ revenue from the GLP-1 fallout, intense competition from other telemedicine providers like Ro and Amazon’s healthcare unit, tighter controls on prescription-based digital healthcare, rising customer acquisition cost, marketing dependence, high valuation, and execution risk.

data.title: Ally Financial: Planned Credit Card Business Sale Shows Renewed Focus On Core Business
data.description:
The past few years have been a golden time for credit card companies. We've seen new high after new high for companies like Visa (V) and Discover (DFS). Meanwhile, one of the smaller players in that industry, Ally Financial (NYSE:ALLY), just last month announced their intention to sell their credit card business to CardWorks.

As you can see, Ally is still a fair distance from the 52-week highs other credit card firms enjoyed, though they are a few months removed from their 52-week lows as well. I thought it would be interesting to take a closer look at Ally, their recent struggles, and why they decided to get out of the credit card business at a time when others are doing so well in it.

The answer is that Ally intends to focus more on its core businesses, which centers on car loans, but also what amounts to America's largest all-digital bank. Even once they finish selling off the credit cards, Ally has quite a few different side businesses in their portfolio, and the hope is that this renewed focus on their core operations will allow them to recover from what has been a struggle in the inflationary environment of the last couple of years.

Trading Below Book

People continue to tell me that I focus too much on balance sheets in judging stocks. I'm a math guy, I can't help it, and in the case of a financial company like Ally, I feel like the balance sheet gives us some very good insights into what this diverse company looks like at present.

Cash and Equivalents

(source: most recent 10-K from SEC)

Whether it's deposits from customers, credit card loans, or car loans, much of Ally's business is centered around credit. Even once they divest of the credit card business, that basic fact isn't going to change. The debt/equity ratio of 12.80 does suggest they're rather highly leveraged at present, though their long-term debt/equity ratio is fairly modest.

The price/book ratio, which of course isn't the end-all, be-all for valuation, is also quite low, 0.84 at present prices. The financial sector leans toward low price/book ratios at present, but Ally is even lower still, with the sector median 1.30.

A discount to book always attracts my attention, personally, even though there generally are reasons for it. Ally's low price/book ratio, in my opinion, is in no small part a function of a market sentiment that is the result of earnings struggles in the last two years.

A Weak 2024 for Earnings, But a Cash Flow Silver Lining

For pure credit card plays, the high interest rates and inflationary environment led to boom times. For Ally, with its deeper connection to vehicle loans, it's a different story. Car loans financed through deposits work differently as a business model than general credit card debt companies, seeing less near-term boost from higher interest rates. Expenses, however, still grow as those rates rise.

Ally has been progressively growing their business, but the interest rate environment just wasn't conducive to that, leading to a better bottom line. In 2024, Ally sourced another $39 billion in loans from 14.6 million new car financing applications. That's a record for them, and speaks to the business continuing apace, even if it didn't translate to as much net income.

There are encouraging signs about the strength of those new applicants, as 44% of them were from the highest credit tier. They should be relatively secure debt, and hopefully that will mean the provisions for losses won't need to be so high in the future.

The corporate finance and banking business is also doing well in adding more depositors, adding 230,000 to that franchise in 2024. According to their conference call, nearly 40% of those depositors are what they consider "engaged savers." They view engaged savers as less sensitive to interest rates in continuing to add to their deposits, which hopefully will mean as interest rates start to come down, they'll continue to add money on that side of things, allowing Ally to issue even more loans.

On a GAAP basis, the P/E ratio is 20.97, above the sector median of 13.29. The non-GAAP P/E is 16.06, which is also a bit above the sector median of 12.30. I view both of these cases as reflective of the interest rate environment in 2024, which was great for some parts of the financial sector, but not so great for others. From a cash flow perspective, though, 2024 wasn't nearly so grim.
